content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive-Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5862          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444


December 21, 1981


Melinda Luedtke
Director
Department of Economic
and Business Development
1030 13th Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Melinda:

     In response to your questions: If an out-of-state trip
for the director of your office of international trade is paid
for by a group of businesses, he or she would have to report
such a trip as a gift or income received from such businesses
on his or her annual Statement of Economic Interests. If a
foreign government pays for such a trip that too would have to be
disclosed as a gift or income. See Sections 87207, 87300, and
87313 of the Government Code, copies attached.

     In addition, if the trip is worth $250 or more, then the
director of the international trade office would be required to
disqualify himself or herself from participating in any decision
which would have a material financial effect - distinguishable
from the public generally -- on the businesses or foreign govern-
ment involved. For example, he or she could not participate
in negotiations which would lead to higher quotas, less shipping
expenses, higher prices, etc. for the commodity produced by the
companies involved in the gift. See Government Code Sections 87100
and 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, copies attached.

     I hope this information is helpful. You may also wish to
consult the guidelines for out-of-state travel promulgated by
the Department of Finance.

                              Sincerely,

                              [signature]
                              Tom K. Houston
                              Chairman

TKH:nrw

sections.question_synthetic: Must a director of an office of international trade disclose and report out-of-state trips paid for by businesses or foreign governments, and what conflict of interest restrictions apply?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Paid out-of-state trips must be reported as gifts or income on the annual Statement of Economic Interests. If a trip is worth $250 or more, the director must disqualify himself/herself from participating in decisions that would have a material financial effect on the paying businesses or foreign government.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal question/conclusion sections present; extracted from letter body. Clear FPPC response with legal citations provided. Document ID and year provided in header.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must a director of an office of international trade disclose and report out-of-state trips paid for by businesses or foreign governments, and what conflict of interest restrictions apply?

CONCLUSION: Paid out-of-state trips must be reported as gifts or income on the annual Statement of Economic Interests. If a trip is worth $250 or more, the director must disqualify himself/herself from participating in decisions that would have a material financial effect on the paying businesses or foreign government.
embedding.first_500_words: December 21, 1981 Melinda Luedtke Director Department of Economic and Business Development 1030 13th Street Sacramento, CA 95814 Dear Melinda: In response to your questions: If an out-of-state trip for the director of your office of international trade is paid for by a group of businesses, he or she would have to report such a trip as a gift or income received from such businesses on his or her annual Statement of Economic Interests. If a foreign government pays for such a trip that too would have to be disclosed as a gift or income. See Sections 87207, 87300, and 87313 of the Government Code, copies attached. In addition, if the trip is worth $250 or more, then the director of the international trade office would be required to disqualify himself or herself from participating in any decision which would have a material financial effect -- distinguishable from the public generally -- on the businesses or foreign government involved. For example, he or she could not participate in negotiations which would lead to higher quotas, less shipping expenses, higher prices, etc. for the commodity produced by the companies involved in the gift. See Government Code Sections 87100 and 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702, copies attached. I hope this information is helpful. You may also wish to consult the guidelines for out-of-state travel promulgated by the Department of Finance. Sincerely, Tom K. Houston Chairman
embedding.summary: FPPC advises that out-of-state trips for an international trade office director paid by businesses or foreign governments must be disclosed as gifts/income and may trigger conflict of interest disqualification requirements if valued at $250 or more.
